pattern: Counting numbers with all-distinct digits reduces to counting, for each possible digit-length, how many ways a non-zero leading digit can be followed by a shrinking pool of still-unused digits — a permutation-counting argument rather than checking each number one at a time.
hints: Checking every integer from 0 up to 10^n - 1 individually for repeated digits is wasteful — think about counting how many valid numbers exist for each digit-length instead of testing each number.

For numbers with exactly k digits (k >= 2), the leading digit has 9 choices (1-9, no leading zero), the next position has 9 remaining choices (any unused digit including 0), the one after that has 8, and so on — the pool of usable digits shrinks by one at each subsequent position.

Start an accumulator at 10 (covering the 10 one-digit values 0-9), then for each additional length from 2 up to n, multiply the previous length's count of arrangements by the shrinking pool size to get this length's count, and add it into the running total.

edge_inputs.description: n=0 — only x=0 itself is in range
n=1 — all single digits 0-9 are trivially unique
n=2 — classic off-by-one: answer is 91, not 100 (00 is not a valid 2-digit number and duplicates like 11 are excluded)
n=4 — the shrinking-pool multiplier must keep decreasing correctly across lengths
n=7 — just below the constraint ceiling
n=8 — constraint ceiling, all ten digits can appear in the longest arrangements

stress.description: sweep the full (tiny) domain 0-8 via a seeded random pick, exercising the same code path as a fuzz check
